description: A 'komalu' is a soft or yielding support placed under the head or another part of the body during sleep and rest. 'nuwera' (bed) is the prepared sleeping place; the pillow supports one smaller point where the resting body needs it. A cushion used as the place for sitting remains 'meipa' (seat).
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into true-mid rounded 'o'. Bring the lips together for voiced nasal 'm', keep air moving through the nose, and open into stressed central 'a'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l', release along its sides, and finish on high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: 'koma' (head) remains whole at the front. Closed lips soften the stressed centre before final 'lu' lets the tongue release into a deep rounded vowel. The word settles lower as the head settles onto support.
usage_notes: Pillows are countable, and ordinary modifiers describe their fill, firmness, condition, or owner. The noun promises support rather than comfort; the body supplies that judgement.

examples.phi: mia nia ha komalu koma thinu.
examples.translation: I put my head on this pillow.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A pillow can prepare the body for sleep without commanding sleep to come. Rest remains an event of the body rather than a property of the object.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Straw or wool could fill a pillow, as could feathers or folded cloth. A household could air and clean the support, then renew the filling when needed.

semantic_domains.physical: a yielding support for the head or another resting body part
semantic_domains.spatial: a small support placed within a sleeping or resting place
